title: Samsung Strike Looms Over AI Chip Supply as Export Controls Stay Off Agenda
subhead: A potential 18-day strike at Samsung threatens global memory chip supply just as the AI boom intensifies, while US-China talks sidestep export controls and Iran's undersea cable threats reveal new digital vulnerabilities.

sections.heading: Samsung Faces Historic Strike Over AI Profit Dispute
sections.body:
More than 45,000 Samsung Electronics workers are threatening an 18-day strike starting May 21, in what would become the largest strike in the company's history. The dispute centers on bonus distribution: workers in the memory-chip division were offered substantially higher bonuses than colleagues in logic and foundry operations, exposing a rift over who benefits from the AI boom.

Samsung has begun 'warm-down' operations to reduce semiconductor production in advance of the walkout. The company warned that the strike could worsen chip shortages and threaten global supply chains. Memory chips are critical for AI data centers, and Samsung is the world's largest memory producer.

South Korea's Prime Minister publicly urged both sides to reach a deal, citing national economic concerns. SK Hynix, Samsung's rival, recently crossed a $1 trillion market cap milestone, intensifying pressure on Samsung to retain talent and maintain its competitive position.
sections.whyItMatters: The strike exposes a fundamental tension in the AI economy: record profits for chipmakers don't automatically translate to worker compensation. Memory chips are the hidden infrastructure of AI—shortages could cascade into data center delays and higher costs for AI services worldwide.

sections.heading: US-China Talks Sidestep Chip Export Controls
sections.body:
US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer told Bloomberg TV that semiconductor export controls were 'not a major topic' in US-China talks, despite ongoing tensions over AI technology transfer. The statement signals that both sides are prioritizing broader trade stability over immediate resolution of chip restrictions.

Separately, the Dutch government objected to proposed US legislation that would further restrict exports to China by ASML, the Dutch lithography equipment maker. The Netherlands expressed concerns about unilateral US measures that could affect Dutch companies without European consultation.

China criticized the proposed US chip equipment bill ahead of the Trump-Xi summit, with Beijing pushing back against restrictions that could limit Chinese chipmakers in the global AI race. Nvidia's H200 chip sales to China remain in limbo as export control discussions stall.
sections.whyItMatters: Export controls remain a background friction point rather than a front-burner issue in US-China relations. The Dutch objection highlights growing tension between US unilateralism and European economic interests in semiconductor equipment.

sections.heading: Pentagon's 'Deal Team Six' Targets Rare Earth Dependence
sections.body:
A Pentagon unit nicknamed 'Deal Team Six'—officially the Economic Defense Unit—is racing to build alternatives to Chinese rare earth supplies. The group, staffed by former Wall Street financiers, has $200 billion in financing capacity over three years to create independent supply chains for critical minerals.

Since China restricted rare earth exports last year in retaliation for US tariffs—forcing the Trump administration to back down—the Pentagon has accelerated deals including a $400 million equity investment in MP Materials, the only US rare earth producer, and a $2.8 billion acquisition of a Brazilian rare earth miner by a US company.

The administration aims to produce enough magnets for half of global demand by 2030. China currently produces 94% of rare earth magnets, a dominance Beijing spent decades building.
sections.whyItMatters: Rare earths are to advanced manufacturing what oil was to the 20th century economy. The Pentagon is essentially doing industrial policy at wartime speed, but critics warn that rushing deals risks backing unproven companies and creating new vulnerabilities.

sections.heading: Iran's Undersea Cable Threats Expose Digital Chokepoints
sections.body:
Iranian state-linked media floated a plan to charge access fees for undersea internet cables passing through the Strait of Hormuz, exploiting what Tehran claims is its offshore territory. The threat follows earlier warnings about vulnerable cables in the strategic waterway.

More than 500 undersea cables carry over 95% of international data traffic. Critical cables including FALCON (Tata Communications), Gulf Bridge, and Tata-TGN Gulf pass through Hormuz, connecting India, Europe, and the Gulf states.

The US-Iran conflict has already delayed construction of new undersea cables in the Persian Gulf. A 2024 Red Sea incident disrupted roughly 25% of internet traffic between Europe and Asia, demonstrating the fragility of concentrated infrastructure.

For India, which relies heavily on Hormuz routes, disruption could slow internet speeds, disrupt cloud services like AWS and Azure, and affect financial trading systems dependent on low-latency connections.
sections.whyItMatters: Digital infrastructure is the new terrain of geopolitical leverage. Unlike conventional attacks, cable sabotage is ambiguous—hard to attribute and legally murky—making it an attractive asymmetric weapon for states seeking pressure without escalation to open conflict.

sections.heading: SpaceX Prepares Starship V3 for Critical Test
sections.body:
SpaceX announced Starship V3 will fly as early as May 19, marking the debut of the newest version of the world's largest and most powerful rocket. At 408 feet tall, V3 features increased propellant capacity, redesigned grid fins, and upgrades targeting full reusability.

The rocket includes a new cryogenic fuel management system crucial for orbital refueling—a technology SpaceX has yet to demonstrate but needs for Moon missions. NASA estimates 17-19 tanker launches would be required to refuel Starship HLS for lunar missions, while Musk has claimed only eight.

The test comes as Blue Origin's Mk1 lunar lander prepares for an uncrewed lunar landing later this year. NASA chief Jared Isaacman stated that 'whichever lander is available first to ensure America achieves its strategic objectives on the Moon is the one we are going to go with.'
sections.whyItMatters: Starship's development isn't just about Mars—it's central to NASA's Artemis program and US lunar ambitions. A failure would strengthen Blue Origin's position, while success positions SpaceX for government contracts and commercial satellite deployment at unprecedented scale.

sections.heading: AI Chip Demand Strains Labor and Supply Chains
sections.body:
Gartner forecasts worldwide semiconductor revenue will exceed $1.3 trillion in 2026, up 64%, with memory revenue expected to triple. DRAM prices are projected to rise 125% and NAND flash prices 234%, with relief not expected until late 2027.

The semiconductor industry will need more than one million additional skilled workers by 2030, according to Deloitte. The US alone faces a gap of 67,000 unfilled positions in technicians, engineers, and computer scientists at current degree completion rates.

Chipmakers are diverting capacity toward high-bandwidth memory for AI servers, creating shortages for conventional DRAM used in PCs and smartphones. Samsung has warned that AI demand is worsening shortages and creating headwinds for its own consumer electronics divisions.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI infrastructure buildout is behaving like critical infrastructure—with chokepoints in labor, memory, power, and manufacturing that can't be automated away. Governments can fund fabs, but they can't instantly create experienced engineers.

bottomLine: Samsung's strike threat is the most immediate risk: memory chips are the oxygen of AI systems, and a prolonged walkout would squeeze global supply just as data center demand peaks. Meanwhile, US-China coordination on export controls remains stalled, rare earth dependency is being addressed through Pentagon industrial policy, and Iran is testing a new form of digital coercion through undersea cable infrastructure. The throughline is infrastructure fragility—whether labor, minerals, or fiber optics, the systems underlying technological power are proving more vulnerable than assumed.
sources: Reuters, Forbes, The Conversation, Outlook Business, Mining.com, Interesting Engineering, Gartner, Deloitte
